intro: Python serves as a versatile language prominent in data tasks, scripting, and enterprise backend engineering, whereas PHP is an execution engine specifically tailored for rapid server-side web development and content delivery. Porting Python code to PHP unifies backend APIs within mainstream web hosting ecosystems, eliminating complex application server routing stacks. This transition swaps indentation-driven processing logic for dynamic web scripting structures designed around stateless HTTP request-response cycles.

diffs.from: Strong, dynamic typing. Prevents silent runtime operations between conflicting types without explicit variable conversions.
diffs.to: Gradual, dynamic typing. Supports strict mode configuration, return type hints, scalar declarations, and automatic string coercion paths.

diffs.from: Indentation-enforced blocks with high-level keyword constructs, avoiding structural tokens or terminal operators.
diffs.to: C-style structural syntax requiring variable dollar signs ($), statement semicolons, curly braces, and string interpolation dots.

diffs.from: Automated object collection managed via internal reference counters and background cyclic sweep loops.
diffs.to: Stateless memory allocation model optimized to teardown and recycle all allocated request references immediately upon request completion.

diffs.from: Native asynchronous concurrency supported by async/await syntax and managed by an internal event loop engine.
diffs.to: Synchronous, single-threaded execution design by nature. Relies on external multi-process managers (FPM) or specialized loop libraries (Swoole).

diffs.from: Extensive standard toolkit offering comprehensive libraries for math processing, networking protocols, and system scripting data models.
diffs.to: Rich, web-centric built-in function catalog engineered for direct cookie management, query handling, and HTML manipulation.

examples.title: Merging arrays and extracting values
examples.description: Compares merging elements and printing outcomes using dictionary concepts versus associative arrays.

examples.title: Declaring a class with type annotations
examples.description: Contrasts a standard Python typed initializer class with a PHP 8 constructor promotion structure.
examples.toCode:
<?php
class Client {
    public function __construct(
        public int $cid,
        public string $role
    ) {}
}

tips.title: Enable Strict Type Validation
tips.body: Declare declare(strict_types=1); at the absolute top of your PHP files to enforce strict compile-time and runtime variable type validation rules.

tips.title: Leverage Constructor Property Promotion
tips.body: Use PHP 8+ constructor visibility keywords to declare and assign properties inline inside class parameters, minimizing structural boilerplate.

tips.title: Convert Dicts to Associative Arrays
tips.body: Python dictionaries map directly to PHP associative arrays. Use brackets with fat arrows (key => value) to implement identical map workflows.

tips.title: Mind the Shared-Nothing Lifecycle
tips.body: Remember that PHP scripts reset completely after each HTTP request. Use persistent caches like Redis to share data state between page refreshes.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of Python's f-strings in PHP?
faqs.answer: PHP supports variable interpolation inside double-quoted strings natively using the variable syntax format directly: `"Hello {$name}"`.

faqs.question: How do PHP namespaces compare to Python imports?
faqs.answer: Python uses file-system hierarchy imports directly. PHP uses virtual `namespace` declarations combined with PSR-4 autoloading configurations managed via Composer.

faqs.question: Does PHP support decorator syntax like Python?
faqs.answer: PHP 8+ includes 'Attributes', which function identically to decorators for attaching structured metadata tags to classes, methods, or parameters.
